content.full_text:
SACRAMENTO, 
5 
Dear 


20, 
I 


D. DWIGHT WORDEN 
W. SCOTT WILLIA 
WORDEN &. WILLIAMS 
ATTORN EYS AT LAW 
POST OFFICE BOX 825 
117 WEST PLAZA, SUITE I 
SOLANA BEACH, CALIFORNIA 92075 
May 5, 1977 
The Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Attention: 
Ken Goshorn 
Dear Mr. Goshorn: 
AREA CODE 714 
TELEPHONE 755-6604 . 459 ·7979 
In conformance with our telephone conversation 
of May 2, 1977, I am writing you this letter requesting 
an informal opinion. 
As we discussed, I am an appointed 
member of the San Diego Coast Regional Commission, having 
been appointed by Governor Brown on January 1, 1977. 
Previous to my appointment I was an attorney in private 
practice. 
A question has now arisen concerning Proposition 
9 and my voting on certain matters as a member of the San 
Diego Coast Regional Commission. 
In 1976 I represented a citizens group in Carlsbad, 
California called "Carlsbad Community Cause", a non-profit, 
unincorporated association of Carlsbad residents. 
My law 
firm represented the group in a lawsuit against the City of 
Carlsbad and the San Diego Gas and Electric Company concerning 
the validity of a referendum petition circulated by the group 
seeking to repeal the City's approval of a 400 foot smokestack 
to be constructed at the San Diego Gas and Electric Company's 
Encina Power Plant. 
As a result of this representation I 
received more than~50.00 in compensation from Carlsbad 
Community Cause within the preceding 12 months. 
Now, an independent developer has applied to the 
San Diego Coast Regional Commission for a permit to construct 
a housing development within the Cit¥of Carlsbad. 
This 
developer is not a member of, and has no connection with, 
Carlsbad Community Cause. 
However, Carlsbad Community Cause 
as an active citizens organization continually gives input 
to various government agencies expressing~~it 
viewpoint 
on significant local projects. 
In this case, Carlsbad Com-
munity Cause testified at the San Diego Coast Regional 
Commission's public hearing on the pending development 
proposal in opposition to the proposal. 
The question then 


The Fair Political Practice Commission 
Attn: Ken Goshorn 
May 5, 1977 
Page 2 
arises since I received more than $250.00 in compensation 
from Carlsbad Community Cause within the preceding 12 months 
am I legally bound to disqualify myself from voting on the 
pending application? 
It seems clear to me that Carlsbad Community 
Cause is a "source of income" within the meaning of the 
law, but it is not so clear that the decision that the 
San Diego Coast Regional Commission makes on the pending 
development proposal may have a material financial effect 
on that source of income. 
It is on this question that I 
seek your assistance. 
It appears to me that a material financial impact 
on Carlsbad Community Cause as a source of income to me is 
not a reasonably forseeable result of our regional com-
missions action on this particular case. 
I have reached 
this conclusion because Carlsbad Community Cause has 
informed me that during 1976 approximately one~third of 
their revenues was generated through dues paid by the 
membership while the remaining two-thirds was generated 
by donations. 
In no case did Carlsbad Community Cause 
solicit donations by advertising or otherwise extolling 
their performance record on opposing or supporting projects. 
Their donations were the result of fund raising events, 
i.e. rummage sales, auctions, international dinners, etc. 
The president of Carlsbad Community Cause further informed 
me that he predicts 1977 revenues will be generated in the 
same fashion and in approximately the same proportion. 
Thus, it appears that there is no material relationship 
between Carlsbad Community Cause's taking a position on 
projects before the Coastal Commission and its ability to 
fund raise. 
Further, since Carlsbad Community Cause owns 
no real property, has no interest in the particular develop-
ment project, and is not a payty to our Coastal Commission 
proceeding but merely appeared to express an opinion at a 
public hearing, it does not appear that a decision by our 
commission to either grant or deny thi:3 particular permit 
will have a material financial effect upon Carlsbad Community 
Cause. 
The question raised by this case involving Carlsbad 
Community Cause is likely to come up again in the future 
I have over the past several years represented various 
citizens groups, ranging from the Sierra Club to small 


The Fair Political Practice Commission 
Attn: Ken Goshorn 
May 5, 1977 
Page 3 
homeowners groups in a particular neighborhood. 
It would be 
very useful to me, and to other members of our commission as 
well, to have some feel for the general guidelines we should 
follow in voting and participating in projects where members 
of groups that were previous clients appear at a public 
hearing. 
I hope that I have adequately raised the issue in 
this letter, and I will be happy to supply you with any 
further information that you need. 
Thank you for your assistance in this matter. 
Sincerely, 
D. DWIGHT WORDEN 
DDW/krf

sections.question: Since I received more than $250.00 in compensation from Carlsbad Community Cause within the preceding 12 months, am I legally bound to disqualify myself from voting on a pending application before the San Diego Coast Regional Commission where Carlsbad Community Cause testified in opposition, given that it is unclear whether the Commission's decision may have a material financial effect on that source of income?
sections.question_synthetic: A Regional Commission member who previously represented a citizens group (Carlsbad Community Cause) for more than $250 in compensation seeks to know whether he must disqualify himself from voting on a development permit application where that former client appeared to testify in opposition, specifically whether the Commission's decision could have a material financial effect on his former client as a source of income.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney to FPPC seeking an informal opinion. No formal FPPC response/conclusion is included in this document. The letter clearly presents the factual background and the legal question regarding conflict of interest under Proposition 9.

embedding.summary: Attorney D. Dwight Worden, an appointed member of the San Diego Coast Regional Commission, requests an informal FPPC opinion regarding whether he must disqualify himself from voting on a housing development application where his former client (Carlsbad Community Cause, from which he received over $250 compensation) testified in opposition, questioning whether the Commission's decision would have a material financial effect on that source of income.
